hints.Accusative: Accusative of 4th declension is also 'actus' (pl.) or 'actum' — here 'actus' is nominative singular subject
hints.Genitive: Genitive singular would be 'actus' (same form in 4th decl.), but here it is the subject paired with 'essendi'
hints.Vocative: This is a metaphysical statement, not an address — 'actus' is nominative

hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns in -us have genitive -i — 'actus, actus' has genitive -us (4th declension)
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have varied endings — 'actus' is 4th declension
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es — 'actus' is 4th declension

hints.to eat: 'to eat' is 'edere' — 'est' here is from 'esse' (to be)

hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sit' — 'est' is indicative
hints.Imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'erat' — 'est' is present indicative

hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat) — 'est' here is from 'esse' (to be)
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand) — a different verb
hints.existo, existere, extiti: These are the parts of 'existere' (to exist) — 'est' is from 'esse'

hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'actualitatem' — 'actualitas' is nominative (predicate noun)
hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 'actualitate' — 'actualitas' is nominative predicate

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'actualitas, actualitatis' with -tas suffix is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'actualitas' is 3rd declension
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es — 'actualitas' is 3rd declension

hints.Nominative: Nominative plural would be 'actus' — 'actuum' with -uum is genitive plural (4th declension)
hints.Accusative: Accusative plural would be 'actus' — 'actuum' is genitive plural
hints.Dative: Dative plural would be 'actibus' — 'actuum' is genitive plural

steps.prompt: What does 'essendi' mean in Thomistic philosophy?
steps.target: Of being / of existing — the gerund of 'esse'
choices: Of being / of existing — the gerund of 'esse'

hints.Of essence: 'Essence' is 'essentia' — 'essendi' is the gerund (genitive) of 'esse', meaning 'of being/existing'
hints.Of sending: 'Of sending' would be 'mittendi' — 'essendi' is from 'esse' (to be)
hints.Of the self: 'Of the self' would use 'sui' — 'essendi' is a verbal noun from 'esse'

translation: The act of being is the actuality of all acts.
